facts.text: Wild coffee plants originated in Ethiopia, but the beverage itself has its roots in Yemen.
Sufi Muslims in the 15th century used coffee to aid concentration during night prayers.
Coffee reached Cairo, Damascus, and Istanbul by the early 16th century.
Coffee was first introduced to Europe in Hungary when the Ottomans invaded at the Battle of Mohács in 1526.
Within a year of reaching Hungary, coffee had reached Vienna via the Ottomans who fought at the Siege of Vienna in 1529.
Coffee was introduced to Malta through slavery — Turkish Muslim slaves imprisoned by the Knights of St John in 1565 used it to make their traditional beverage.
The first mention of coffee in a European text is in Charles de l'Ecluse's work from 1575.
Gabriel de Clieu brought coffee seedlings to Martinique in the Caribbean in 1720.
Fifty years after Gabriel de Clieu's introduction, there were 18,680 coffee trees in Martinique.
By 1788, the French territory of Saint-Domingue (Haiti) supplied half the world's coffee.
The dreadful conditions slaves endured on Saint-Domingue's coffee plantations were a contributing factor to the Haitian Revolution.
For nearly two centuries up to the end of the 17th century, Yemen was the world's sole gateway for coffee.
The English word 'coffee' came from the Ottoman Turkish word 'ḳahve', which was borrowed from the Arabic 'qahwah'.
Medieval Arab lexicographers linked the etymology of 'qahwah' to the word for 'wine' due to coffee's dark color.
The Arabic root 'qhh' meaning 'dark color' is the most likely origin of the word for coffee.
There is no evidence that the word 'qahwah' was named after the Ethiopian province of Kaffa.
In Ethiopian languages, coffee is called 'buna', 'bun', 'būn', or 'buni' depending on the language.
A 2020 genetic study confirmed that the vast majority of all Arabica coffee in the world today originates from early cultivated varieties in Yemen.
Coffea arabica varieties were found to have low genetic diversity.
The original domesticated coffee plant is said to have been from Harar, Ethiopia.
Coffee houses were established in Western Europe by the late 17th century, especially in Holland, England, and Germany.
Coffee entered Europe through Mediterranean trade routes in the mid-16th century, first in Italy.
Gabriel de Clieu's seedlings from Martinique enabled coffee to spread to Saint-Domingue, Mexico, and other Caribbean islands.
Coffee cultivation began in the French territory of Saint-Domingue in 1734.
Coffee reached the Isle of Bourbon (now Réunion) in the Indian Ocean, where it produced smaller beans classified as a different variety called var. Bourbon.
The Santos coffee of Brazil and the Oaxaca coffee of Mexico are both descended from the Bourbon tree of Réunion.
Circa 1727, the King of Portugal sent Francisco de Melo Palheta to French Guiana to obtain coffee seeds.
During early cultivation periods, brewed coffee was reserved exclusively for the priesthood and the medical profession.
The German traveler Gustav Sommerfeldt in 1663 wrote about Turkish prisoners earning money by preparing coffee.
Domenico Magri referred to Turks as 'most skillful makers' of the coffee concoction in his work Virtu del Kafé.
